title: How likely is Iran to carry out a retaliatory cyberattack on the U.S.?
author: NBC News
fullText: The war in Iran is sparking cyber security concerns here in the US. In the past, Iran has launched several digital attacks in the US, including ransomware and data breaches. As of now, hackers linked to Iran have made threats on social media, but so far not any confirmed major cyber attacks. Joining us to talk more about this is NBC News reporter Kevin Collier. Uh Kevin, some Iranian hacker groups have promised, as we said on social media, they're saying, "We're going to retaliate." They said it on Telegram or X. How real is the possibility of Iran taking some action based on history? What kind of capabilities do they have? Well, >> historically, maybe 10, 12 years ago, they did have some pretty substantial ability. They took out um a couple websites entirely. These days, Iran has been a much smaller player for the most part. Uh you know, we're all concerned that maybe they're going to turn out the lights or something like that, but it seems like well, first of all, we have to remember that Iran has almost no internet access from the country right now. And so their ability to actually physically make, you know, make an internet connection at all is pretty limited. Um, and a lot of their military capabilities are also degraded. So we don't really know how many hackers they have in waiting right now who are acting. So they've been going for some like targets of opportunity, you could say. So I took a screen grab um yesterday where they this is there's some Israeli actor playright and he um his website has been defaced by this activist group and it's like I'm feel sorry for this guy u but this is really minor stakes, right? This is there's a war going on and the hacks are pretty minor. >> Right. Right. That's the good news, right? We're not hearing about major things. I do want to ask you about Amazon because yesterday two of its data centers uh in the United Arab Emirates and a third facility in Bahrain were struck by drones. What do we know about this? >> It's remarkable. Right. This is I believe the first time we've ever had the case that you know a major American data center, major American uh you know internet provider has had severely degraded service in in a region in the Middle East. you know, Amazon is saying that we have, you know, much less uh capability to do most of our services right now in this area um because of three drone strikes. Two in, like you said, two two in UAE and one one indirect one in Bahrain. And they say service is not going to fully restore for a very long time. There's massive fires. They have water damage from putting out the fires. Uh and that's just the beginning of this. Yeah. >> Yeah. All right, Kevin Collier for us. Kevin, thank you so much. We thank you for watching and remember, stay updated on breaking news and top stories on the NBC News app [snorts] or watch live on our YouTube channel.
